---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
#format: revealjs
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: true
    pdf-engine: xelatex  # or xelatex
    # mathfont: "Latin Modern Math"
incremental: true
header-includes:
  - \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
---

<!-- the section below is shown only if rendering is beamer from latex. (and format:revealjs is commmented) -->

::: {.content-visible when-format="beamer"}
```{=latex}
% TITLE SLIDES
\title{Clinical Research Methodology\\ \vspace{1em} \emph{3. Test Selection}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{

\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{\today}
\maketitle
```
:::

# Test Selection for Your Toolbox

## Test Selection

Two moments of test selection:

1) **Selection of tests to include in your own "toolbox"**

2) Selection of tests to use with a specific individual for a specific purpose.

## \small Selection of Tests to Include in Your "Toolbox"

*General rules to follow*

Tests that have satisfactory psychometric characteristics (validity, reliability, adequate normative data, \underline{see following slides})

It depends very much on practical factors: the type of clinical setting in which you use tests influences which tests you should potentially use.

## \small Selection of Tests to Include in Your "Toolbox"

*How to select tests?*

\underline{Possible sources:}

**Neuropsychologist's Kit**: a book produced by the Italian Society of Neuropsychology (for members) that contains references to almost all tests available in Italian (however, it is not very up-to-date and is hard to find).

**Book "The Neuropsychological Examination of Adults"** (edited by Bianchi A.): contains a chapter that includes a rather extensive list of neuropsychological tests adapted to Italian.


## \small Selection of Tests to Include in Your "Toolbox"

*How to select tests?*

\underline{Possible sources:}

**Test publishing houses (Specialized Organizations, Eriksson)**: these are paid tests that guarantee a certain quality standard.


## \small Selection of Tests to Include in Your "Toolbox"

\begin{center}
  \textbf{METHODS I SUGGEST}
\end{center}

**University instructors, future colleagues and experts in a certain field/pathology**: it is the best way (if you find someone willing to share the information).

**Scientific articles/consensus specific to pathology**: Search (e.g., on scholar or pubmed) if there are guidelines or criteria and check if tests are validated/standardized in Italian.

**Expectations from the clinical world**: it is possible that the clinical world expects certain tests. While the message to use the best possible tests remains, you must also consider that certain tests might be expected.

## \small Selection of Tests to Include in Your "Toolbox"

**Scientific articles/consensus**

Remember that having the scientific article is often not enough. You also need the instruction manual and administration (and scoring) procedures and the test materials, any normative data tables.

Use caution with tests that you do not have official references for (they may have "custom" modifications that alter the test).

*Contact the authors of the test (the email is in the article) and ask if it is available.*

## Test Selection to Include in Your "Toolbox"

*What to avoid in test selection?*

\begin{itemize}[<*>]
  \item Avoid tests that are too old (if the normative data are too old they may not be usable) – \emph{we will explore this further}.
  \item Avoid "homemade" tests: a test passed from a colleague without normative data (perhaps translated from an English test) can be appealing (and sometimes useful) but is limited in the information it can provide. - \emph{we will explore this further}.
  \item Avoid tests that are simple translations from foreign tests, not "adapted" to Italian through proper validity, reliability, and normative data studies.
\end{itemize}

## Criteria for Selecting Tests for Clinical Evaluations

```{=latex}
\begin{columns}
\column{0.7\textwidth}

\textbf{1) Tests appropriate for the potential questions of the evaluations you will perform}

\column{0.3\textwidth}

\begin{figure}
    \includegraphics[scale=0.04]{../Figures/triangle.png}
\end{figure}

\end{columns}
\pause
\vfill

\begin{columns}
\column{1\textwidth}

\textbf{2) Tests with good psychometric capabilities}
\begin{itemize}[<*>]
  \item Tests with good validity
  \item Tests with good reliability
  \item Tests with good properties of other types (e.g., change values)
  \item Tests with adequate normative data.
\end{itemize}

\column{0\textwidth}
\end{columns}

\vfill

In general, do not forget the rationale: tests provide us with information, and part of that information comes from the characteristics of the tests.
```

## Test Selection

![](../Figures/Active_collection.png)
